trialDesign.fullName: Liraglutide Effect and Action in Diabetes: Evaluation of Cardiovascular Outcome Results (LEADER)
trialDesign.design: Multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled cardiovascular outcomes trial
trialDesign.population: 9,340 patients with type 2 diabetes at high cardiovascular risk; mean HbA1c 8.7%
trialDesign.intervention: Liraglutide up to 1.8 mg SC once daily plus standard care
trialDesign.comparator: Placebo plus standard care (DPP-4 inhibitors forbidden as add-on)
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: First occurrence of CV death, nonfatal MI, or nonfatal stroke
trialDesign.overview: Liraglutide reduced primary MACE by 13% (HR 0.87), CV death by 22%, all-cause death by 15%, and microvascular events by 16%.

headings.background: Regulatory mandate for CV safety of diabetes drugs; liraglutide GLP-1 analogue efficacy established but long-term CV outcomes unknown.
headings.design: 9340 patients; 2-week placebo run-in; stratified by eGFR; hierarchical noninferiority then superiority testing.
headings.population: 72.4% established CVD; >24% CKD stage 3+; high CV risk type 2 diabetes.
headings.comparator: Placebo with standard glycemic and CV therapy.

safetyHighlights: GI events drove discontinuation; gallstone disease more common
No medullary thyroid carcinoma; pancreatitis not increased; severe hypoglycemia reduced
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: Liraglutide reduces cardiovascular and all-cause mortality in high-risk type 2 diabetes with nephropathy benefit and acceptable safety profile.

inclusionCriteria: Type 2 diabetes at high cardiovascular risk
Age criteria per protocol with established CVD or CV risk factors
exclusionCriteria: Personal or family history of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 or medullary thyroid carcinoma
DPP-4 inhibitor use as protocol-defined add-on restriction
keyTakeaways: Primary MACE HR 0.87; CV death HR 0.78; all-cause death HR 0.85.
Microvascular HR 0.84 driven by nephropathy; HF hospitalization nonsignificantly lower.
Benefits emerge gradually vs empagliflozin; gallstones ↑; severe hypoglycemia ↓.

items.title: LEADER — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Objective: Liraglutide is a GLP-1 analogue for type 2 diabetes; regulatory authorities require cardiovascular safety assessment. LEADER evaluated long-term cardiovascular safety and efficacy in high-risk type 2 diabetes.

Methodology: Multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial; 9,340 patients randomized to liraglutide (up to 1.8 mg daily) or placebo plus standard care. Primary composite: CV death, nonfatal MI, or nonfatal stroke.

Primary Cardiovascular Outcomes: Median follow-up 3.8 years; primary composite reduced 13% (HR 0.87); noninferiority and superiority achieved.

Mortality and Secondary Outcomes: CV death ↓22%; all-cause death ↓15%; nonfatal MI, stroke, and HF hospitalization numerically lower but individually nonsignificant.

Microvascular and Safety Profile: Microvascular events ↓ (nephropathy-driven); GI events most common cause of discontinuation; gallstones ↑; no significant pancreatitis ↑; severe hypoglycemia ↓.
keyPoints: Primary composite HR 0.87 · CV death HR 0.78 · all-cause HR 0.85
Microvascular HR 0.84 (nephropathy); NNT 98 over 3 years for one death
GI discontinuations; gallstones ↑; severe hypoglycemia ↓; no MTC cases
items.reference: Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why was the LEADER trial initiated despite liraglutide already possessing established glucose-lowering efficacy?
items.answer: The trial was initiated to satisfy regulatory mandates requiring robust cardiovascular safety assessments for new diabetes treatments, given historical concerns that some antihyperglycemic therapies might inadvertently increase cardiovascular risk.
keyPoints: The trial was initiated to satisfy regulatory mandates requiring robust cardiovascular safety assessments for new diabetes treatments, given historical concerns that some antihyperglycemic therapies might inadvertently increase cardiovascular risk.
items.reference: LEADER Q1: The trial was initiated to satisfy regulatory mandates requiring robust cardiova... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How was the primary statistical hypothesis of the study structured to evaluate safety and efficacy?
items.answer: The trial utilized a hierarchical testing strategy that first tested for noninferiority (requiring the upper limit of the 95% confidence interval of the hazard ratio to be less than 1.30). Once noninferiority was established, the model subsequently tested for statistical superiority.
keyPoints: The trial utilized a hierarchical testing strategy that first tested for noninferiority (requiring the upper limit of the 95% confidence interval of the hazard ratio to be less than 1.30). Once noninferiority was established, the model subsequently tested for statistical superiority.
items.reference: LEADER Q2: The trial utilized a hierarchical testing strategy that first tested for noninfe... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why do the authors suggest the cardiovascular benefits of liraglutide differ mechanistically from those of empagliflozin (an SGLT2 inhibitor)?
items.answer: Unlike the EMPA-REG OUTCOME trial where benefits emerged very early (suggesting rapid hemodynamic changes), the benefits in the LEADER trial emerged more gradually over time. The authors hypothesize this slower divergence reflects the modified progression of atherosclerotic vascular disease rather than acute fluid shifts.
keyPoints: Unlike the EMPA-REG OUTCOME trial where benefits emerged very early (suggesting rapid hemodynamic changes), the benefits in the LEADER trial emerged more gradually over time. The authors hypothesize this slower divergence reflects the modified progression of atherosclerotic vascular disease rather than acute fluid shifts.
items.reference: LEADER Q3: Unlike the EMPA-REG OUTCOME trial where benefits emerged very early (suggesting ... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did baseline renal function influence the primary cardiovascular outcome in the subgroup analyses?
items.answer: Interaction testing revealed that patients with an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of less than 60 ml/min/1.73 m² experienced a significantly greater cardiovascular benefit from liraglutide than those with an eGFR of 60 or higher.
keyPoints: Interaction testing revealed that patients with an estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of less than 60 ml/min/1.73 m² experienced a significantly greater cardiovascular benefit from liraglutide than those with an eGFR of 60 or higher.
items.reference: LEADER Q4: Interaction testing revealed that patients with an estimated glomerular filtrati... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why was calcitonin systematically monitored throughout the duration of the trial?
items.answer: Preclinical studies in rodents demonstrated that GLP-1 receptor agonists activate thyroid C-cells, causing calcitonin release and proliferation. Researchers monitored calcitonin to ensure liraglutide did not increase the risk of medullary thyroid carcinoma in humans (no cases were observed in the liraglutide group).
keyPoints: Preclinical studies in rodents demonstrated that GLP-1 receptor agonists activate thyroid C-cells, causing calcitonin release and proliferation. Researchers monitored calcitonin to ensure liraglutide did not increase the risk of medullary thyroid carcinoma in humans (no cases were observed in the liraglutide group).
items.reference: LEADER Q5: Preclinical studies in rodents demonstrated that GLP-1 receptor agonists activat... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did the intervention impact the patients' composite microvascular outcomes?
items.answer: Liraglutide significantly lowered the composite outcome of renal and retinal microvascular events (HR 0.84), a benefit driven exclusively by a significant reduction in nephropathy, particularly new-onset persistent macroalbuminuria.
keyPoints: Liraglutide significantly lowered the composite outcome of renal and retinal microvascular events (HR 0.84), a benefit driven exclusively by a significant reduction in nephropathy, particularly new-onset persistent macroalbuminuria.
items.reference: LEADER Q6: Liraglutide significantly lowered the composite outcome of renal and retinal mic... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why might the incidence of severe hypoglycemia have been significantly lower in the liraglutide group compared to the placebo group?
items.answer: Because liraglutide effectively lowered blood glucose independently, patients in the liraglutide group required fewer add-on hypoglycemic medications—specifically insulin and sulfonylureas, which are known drivers of severe hypoglycemic episodes—than patients in the placebo group.
keyPoints: Because liraglutide effectively lowered blood glucose independently, patients in the liraglutide group required fewer add-on hypoglycemic medications—specifically insulin and sulfonylureas, which are known drivers of severe hypoglycemic episodes—than patients in the placebo group.
items.reference: LEADER Q7: Because liraglutide effectively lowered blood glucose independently, patients in... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did liraglutide clinically alter the patients' basic cardiovascular vital signs?
items.answer: At 36 months, liraglutide was associated with a 1.2 mm Hg reduction in systolic blood pressure and a 0.6 mm Hg reduction in diastolic blood pressure, but it simultaneously caused an increase in the resting heart rate by 3.0 beats per minute.
keyPoints: At 36 months, liraglutide was associated with a 1.2 mm Hg reduction in systolic blood pressure and a 0.6 mm Hg reduction in diastolic blood pressure, but it simultaneously caused an increase in the resting heart rate by 3.0 beats per minute.
items.reference: LEADER Q8: At 36 months, liraglutide was associated with a 1.2 mm Hg reduction in systolic ... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why did the researchers specify a 2-week placebo run-in phase prior to randomization?
items.answer: This run-in phase was utilized to establish medication tolerance and ensure that patients were able to reliably adhere to the daily subcutaneous injection regimen before being formally randomized into the trial.
keyPoints: This run-in phase was utilized to establish medication tolerance and ensure that patients were able to reliably adhere to the daily subcutaneous injection regimen before being formally randomized into the trial.
items.reference: LEADER Q9: This run-in phase was utilized to establish medication tolerance and ensure that... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did the investigators address the theoretical risk of acute pancreatitis associated with incretin therapies?
items.answer: They utilized an external, independent event-adjudication committee to review outcomes blindly. The trial ultimately showed a numerically lower rate of acute pancreatitis in the liraglutide group (18 cases) compared to the placebo group (23 cases).
keyPoints: They utilized an external, independent event-adjudication committee to review outcomes blindly. The trial ultimately showed a numerically lower rate of acute pancreatitis in the liraglutide group (18 cases) compared to the placebo group (23 cases).
items.reference: LEADER Q10: They utilized an external, independent event-adjudication committee to review ou... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why did significantly more patients in the liraglutide group permanently discontinue their trial regimen compared to placebo?
items.answer: Discontinuation was driven primarily by adverse gastrointestinal events, specifically significantly higher rates of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea in the liraglutide cohort.
keyPoints: Discontinuation was driven primarily by adverse gastrointestinal events, specifically significantly higher rates of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea in the liraglutide cohort.
items.reference: LEADER Q11: Discontinuation was driven primarily by adverse gastrointestinal events, specifi... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did the "expanded composite outcome" differ conceptually from the primary composite outcome?
items.answer: While the primary outcome focused on hard endpoints (CV death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke), the expanded composite outcome added clinical utilization metrics, specifically coronary revascularization and hospitalization for unstable angina pectoris or heart failure.
keyPoints: While the primary outcome focused on hard endpoints (CV death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke), the expanded composite outcome added clinical utilization metrics, specifically coronary revascularization and hospitalization for unstable angina pectoris or heart failure.
items.reference: LEADER Q12: While the primary outcome focused on hard endpoints (CV death, nonfatal MI, nonf... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why was the finding regarding hospitalization for heart failure notable within the context of diabetes pharmacology?
items.answer: Previous trials involving DPP-4 inhibitors (another class of incretin-based therapy) had raised significant clinical concerns regarding increased risks of heart failure hospitalization. In LEADER, hospitalizations for heart failure were numerically lower with liraglutide, easing this specific safety concern.
keyPoints: Previous trials involving DPP-4 inhibitors (another class of incretin-based therapy) had raised significant clinical concerns regarding increased risks of heart failure hospitalization. In LEADER, hospitalizations for heart failure were numerically lower with liraglutide, easing this specific safety concern.
items.reference: LEADER Q13: Previous trials involving DPP-4 inhibitors (another class of incretin-based ther... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did the incidence of acute gallstone disease differ between the two study groups?
items.answer: Acute gallstone disease was significantly more common in the liraglutide group (145 patients) compared to the placebo group (90 patients), including a higher rate of severe events and acute cholecystitis.
keyPoints: Acute gallstone disease was significantly more common in the liraglutide group (145 patients) compared to the placebo group (90 patients), including a higher rate of severe events and acute cholecystitis.
items.reference: LEADER Q14: Acute gallstone disease was significantly more common in the liraglutide group (... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Why do the authors emphasize that the study cannot confirm or exclude the effect of liraglutide on cancer risk, despite observing an excess of pancreatic cancer cases?
items.answer: Although there were 13 pancreatic cancer cases in the liraglutide group versus 5 in the placebo group, randomized cardiovascular outcome trials—even large ones—are not statistically powered or of sufficient duration to definitively evaluate long-term oncological risks.
keyPoints: Although there were 13 pancreatic cancer cases in the liraglutide group versus 5 in the placebo group, randomized cardiovascular outcome trials—even large ones—are not statistically powered or of sufficient duration to definitively evaluate long-term oncological risks.
items.reference: LEADER Q15: Although there were 13 pancreatic cancer cases in the liraglutide group versus 5... — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What was the maximum daily dose of liraglutide targeted for patients in the intervention group?
items.explanation: Target maximum liraglutide dose was 1.8 mg daily.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 1: Target maximum liraglutide dose was 1.8 mg daily. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What was the Number Needed to Treat (NNT) over 3 years to prevent one death from any cause?
items.explanation: NNT 98 over 3 years to prevent one all-cause death.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 2: NNT 98 over 3 years to prevent one all-cause death. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Which of the following conditions was a major exclusion criterion for entering the LEADER trial?
options: A familial history of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2
items.explanation: MEN2/familial medullary thyroid carcinoma history was excluded.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 3: MEN2/familial medullary thyroid carcinoma history was excluded. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What was the hazard ratio for the primary composite outcome in the liraglutide group?
items.explanation: Primary composite HR 0.87 (13% relative risk reduction).
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 4: Primary composite HR 0.87 (13% relative risk reduction). — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Which specific component of the composite microvascular outcome was found to be nonsignificantly higher in the liraglutide group?
items.explanation: Retinopathy events were nonsignificantly higher with liraglutide.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 5: Retinopathy events were nonsignificantly higher with liraglutide. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: At the prespecified 36-month mark, what was the mean difference in body weight between the liraglutide and placebo groups?
options: 0.6 kg greater loss in the liraglutide group
2.3 kg greater loss in the liraglutide group
5.0 kg greater loss in the liraglutide group
There was no significant difference in weight
items.explanation: Liraglutide group lost 2.3 kg more than placebo at 36 months.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 6: Liraglutide group lost 2.3 kg more than placebo at 36 months. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did liraglutide affect the rate of death from cardiovascular causes?
options: It reduced the relative risk by 13% (HR 0.87).
It reduced the relative risk by 22% (HR 0.78).
It had no statistically significant effect on CV death.
It increased the rate of CV death compared to placebo.
items.explanation: CV death reduced 22% (HR 0.78).
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 7: CV death reduced 22% (HR 0.78). — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Which drug class was explicitly forbidden as an add-on therapy for glycemic control during the trial?
items.explanation: DPP-4 inhibitors were forbidden as add-on therapy.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 8: DPP-4 inhibitors were forbidden as add-on therapy. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: The baseline stratification of patients during randomization was based on which clinical parameter?
items.explanation: Randomization stratified by baseline eGFR.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 9: Randomization stratified by baseline eGFR. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What was the median duration of follow-up for the trial cohort?
items.explanation: Median follow-up was 3.8 years.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 10: Median follow-up was 3.8 years. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Which baseline patient subgroup demonstrated a statistically significant interaction indicating a greater benefit from liraglutide regarding the primary outcome?
options: Patients with established cardiovascular disease at baseline
items.explanation: Established CVD at baseline showed significant interaction favoring liraglutide.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 11: Established CVD at baseline showed significant interaction favoring liraglutide. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What was the mean baseline glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level of the enrolled patients?
items.explanation: Mean baseline HbA1c was 8.7%.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 12: Mean baseline HbA1c was 8.7%. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How did serum amylase and lipase levels behave in the liraglutide group compared to placebo?
options: The mean levels were higher in the liraglutide group.
The mean levels were significantly lower in the liraglutide group.
They dropped dramatically to unmeasurable levels.
items.explanation: Mean amylase and lipase were higher with liraglutide.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 13: Mean amylase and lipase were higher with liraglutide. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: The hazard ratio for death from noncardiovascular causes in the trial was 0.95. What was the P value for this specific outcome?
items.explanation: Noncardiovascular death HR 0.95; P=0.66 (not significant).
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 14: Noncardiovascular death HR 0.95; P=0.66 (not significant). — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Which trial evaluated the structurally dissimilar, short-acting GLP-1 agonist lixisenatide and found NO cardiovascular benefit?
items.explanation: ELIXA evaluated lixisenatide without CV benefit.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 15: ELIXA evaluated lixisenatide without CV benefit. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What was the overall completion rate for patients reaching a final visit, dying, or experiencing a primary outcome?
items.explanation: 96.8% reached final visit, death, or primary outcome.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 16: 96.8% reached final visit, death, or primary outcome. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: According to the study parameters, what margin for the upper boundary of the 95% CI was required to prove noninferiority?
items.explanation: Noninferiority required upper 95% CI <1.30.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 17: Noninferiority required upper 95% CI <1.30. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: How many cases of medullary thyroid carcinoma were confirmed in the liraglutide group?
items.explanation: Zero medullary thyroid carcinoma cases in liraglutide group.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 18: Zero medullary thyroid carcinoma cases in liraglutide group. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: Severe hypoglycemia was defined as hypoglycemia requiring:
options: A plasma glucose level strictly below 56 mg/dL
items.explanation: Severe hypoglycemia required third-party assistance.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 19: Severe hypoglycemia required third-party assistance. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.question: What percentage of the overall study population had established cardiovascular disease at baseline?
items.explanation: 72.4% had established cardiovascular disease at baseline.
items.reference: LEADER MCQ 20: 72.4% had established cardiovascular disease at baseline. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: Liraglutide demonstrated statistically significant superiority over placebo in reducing the individual rate of nonfatal myocardial infarctions.
items.explanation: Nonfatal MI was numerically lower but not statistically significant.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 1: Nonfatal MI was numerically lower but not statistically significant. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The LEADER trial was sponsored and funded primarily by Novo Nordisk and the National Institutes of Health.
items.explanation: Funded by Novo Nordisk with NIH support.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 2: Funded by Novo Nordisk with NIH support. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: Patients in the placebo group were more likely to require the addition of insulin or sulfonylureas to control their blood glucose during the trial.
items.explanation: Placebo group required more insulin and sulfonylureas.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 3: Placebo group required more insulin and sulfonylureas. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The trial strictly excluded patients who had chronic kidney disease of stage 3 or higher.
items.explanation: CKD stage 3+ was not excluded; >24% had CKD stage 3+.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 4: CKD stage 3+ was not excluded; >24% had CKD stage 3+. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: Liraglutide use was associated with a clinically measured increase in the resting heart rate of patients.
items.explanation: Resting heart rate increased ~3 beats/min with liraglutide.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 5: Resting heart rate increased ~3 beats/min with liraglutide. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The incidence of acute pancreatitis was significantly higher in the liraglutide group than in the placebo group.
items.explanation: Pancreatitis numerically lower (18 vs 23); not significantly higher.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 6: Pancreatitis numerically lower (18 vs 23); not significantly higher. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: Patients assigned to the liraglutide group experienced a greater mean reduction in glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) over 36 months compared to the placebo group.
items.explanation: Greater HbA1c reduction with liraglutide at 36 months.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 7: Greater HbA1c reduction with liraglutide at 36 months. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The hierarchical testing model mandated that the researchers test for superiority before establishing noninferiority.
items.explanation: Noninferiority tested first, then superiority.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 8: Noninferiority tested first, then superiority. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The trial demonstrated that liraglutide significantly reduced the risk of hospitalizations for heart failure.
items.explanation: HF hospitalization was nonsignificantly lower.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 9: HF hospitalization was nonsignificantly lower. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: There was a numerical excess of adjudicated pancreatic cancer cases in the liraglutide group compared to the placebo group.
items.explanation: 13 vs 5 pancreatic cancer cases (not powered for cancer conclusions).
items.reference: LEADER T/F 10: 13 vs 5 pancreatic cancer cases (not powered for cancer conclusions). — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: A subgroup analysis showed that patients of Asian descent experienced the largest and most significant harm from taking liraglutide.
items.explanation: Asian subgroup HR 0.70 favored liraglutide, not harm.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 11: Asian subgroup HR 0.70 favored liraglutide, not harm. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: A history of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 was a major exclusion criterion for entering the study.
items.explanation: MEN2 history was an exclusion criterion.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 12: MEN2 history was an exclusion criterion. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The cardiovascular benefits observed in LEADER emerged rapidly within the first 3 months of the trial, mimicking the hemodynamic timeline of SGLT2 inhibitors.
items.explanation: Benefits emerged gradually, unlike early EMPA-REG separation.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 13: Benefits emerged gradually, unlike early EMPA-REG separation. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: Liraglutide administration involves a once-daily subcutaneous injection.
items.explanation: Liraglutide given once daily subcutaneously.
items.reference: LEADER T/F 14: Liraglutide given once daily subcutaneously. — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.

items.statement: The trial completely failed to show any reduction in the rate of death from any cause (all-cause mortality).
items.explanation: All-cause mortality significantly reduced (HR 0.85; 15% RRR).
items.reference: LEADER T/F 15: All-cause mortality significantly reduced (HR 0.85; 15% RRR). — Marso SP, Daniels GH, Brown-Frandsen K, et al. Liraglutide and cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2016;375:311-322.
